note: Social-engineering markers observed in English-language scams. Authored for English, not translated from another language — the tactics that dominate differ by region (gift cards and wire transfers here, 'safe account' transfers in Russian-language fraud). Severity is a contribution to the overall picture, not a verdict. Additions welcome by pull request.

groups.title: Claiming to be an institution
groups.explanation: The sender claims to represent a bank, tax office, or law enforcement. Real institutions do not open these conversations by message or cold call.
markers: your social security number has been suspended

groups.title: Asking for codes or keys
groups.explanation: One-time codes, card security numbers, and recovery phrases are never requested by anyone legitimate. Anyone asking is trying to take the account.

groups.title: Asking for gift cards
groups.explanation: No government agency, utility, or employer accepts gift cards as payment. A request for gift card numbers is fraud with no legitimate exception.
markers: send me the code on the card

groups.title: Pushing an irreversible payment method
groups.explanation: Wire transfers, crypto, and peer-to-peer apps cannot be reversed once sent. Scams steer toward them for exactly that reason.

groups.title: Telling you to keep it quiet
groups.explanation: Being asked to keep something from family, colleagues, or bank staff is isolation. It exists to remove the people who would stop this.

groups.title: Rushing you
groups.explanation: Pressure exists to stop you from checking. A real request survives a night's sleep and a second opinion.

groups.title: Threatening your account or freedom
groups.explanation: Fear of loss shuts down careful thinking. That is the point of the threat.

groups.title: Asking you to pay first
groups.explanation: A fee, tax, or deposit required before you receive money is a standard scam shape. Real payouts do not require you to send money first.

groups.title: Promising returns without risk
groups.explanation: Guaranteed returns do not exist. The more specific the promise, the more likely the scheme.

groups.title: Asking you to install software
groups.explanation: Remote access tools hand over full control of the device, including banking apps. Support you did not call for never needs this.
markers: install this app so i can

groups.title: Overpayment or refund reversal
groups.explanation: Being told you were overpaid and asked to return the difference is a classic setup — the original payment is later reversed and you lose both.

groups.title: Demanding money or making threats
groups.explanation: A demand for money backed by a threat or insult is extortion. Do not reply, and keep the messages — they may be needed as evidence.

groups.title: Manufactured activity and rankings
groups.explanation: Leaderboards, prize tallies and winner announcements from an account you did not subscribe to are engagement bait. The numbers are decoration; the point is to make the operation look busy and legitimate.

groups.title: Pushing you into a private chat
groups.explanation: An unsolicited message that ends by telling you to write privately is usually a funnel. The actual pitch happens where nobody else can see it or warn you.

groups.title: Paid tasks that need a deposit
groups.explanation: Gamified work — liking videos, rating products, 'boosting' listings — with a balance that grows on screen. Eventually you are asked to deposit your own money to unlock or withdraw it. Real employers never make you pay to get paid.

groups.title: A family member with a new number
groups.explanation: The most successful scam against parents: a message claiming to be your child on a borrowed or replaced phone, followed by a money problem. If it really is them, calling their old number settles it in ten seconds — and if it is not, that call is the whole defence.

groups.title: A stranger opening a conversation
groups.explanation: Long investment frauds start with a friendly message that contains no pitch at all — a wrong number, a half-remembered meeting. The only goal is to get you replying. There is no cost to not answering a stranger.
markers: sent this to the wrong person
i think i have the wrong

groups.title: A package or toll that needs paying
groups.explanation: Unpaid tolls and stuck parcels are the most reported text scams. The fee is small on purpose — the goal is your card details, not the money.

groups.title: Offering to recover money you lost
groups.explanation: People who have already been defrauded are sold to other fraudsters as a list. Nobody who contacts you first can recover your money, and every fee they ask for is a second theft.
